brief.name: Lyon Stahl Investment Real Estate
brief.what_they_do: A multifamily investment brokerage serving Los Angeles and Southern California, specializing in acquisitions, dispositions, and 1031 exchanges for multifamily assets. They operate 4 offices with 125+ agents and have closed over $5.9 billion across 3,600+ transactions.

brief.brief: Lyon Stahl Investment Real Estate is a Los Angeles-based multifamily brokerage founded in 2014 by Brett Lyon and Woody Stahl, now operating 4 offices with 125+ agents and claiming #1 multifamily market share in Southern California. The firm has closed $5.9 billion across 3,600+ transactions and has been recognized as a CoStar Power Broker Top Sales Firm (2023–2025), GlobeSt. Influencer in Multifamily (2025), and Inc. 5000 fastest-growing company. They specialize in acquisitions, dispositions, 1031 exchanges, and commercial/luxury real estate across Los Angeles, Long Beach, South Bay, and Inland Empire, positioning themselves as a collaborative, knowledge-sharing alternative to traditional brokerages.

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Lyon Stahl wants to be the undisputed #1 multifamily brokerage in Southern California — not just by volume ($5.9B closed, 3,600+ transactions [transaction_volume]) but by agent count and market reach. Their site language is explicit: they're a 'collaborative, knowledge-sharing alternative to traditional brokerages' [growth_trajectory], and they're actively recruiting agents into a 4-office, 125+ person operation [employee_count]. The goal is scale — more agents closing more deals across LA, Long Beach, South Bay, and Inland Empire [geographic_coverage].

THE OBSTACLE:
Growth at this stage lives or dies on two things: getting the right leads in front of the right agents fast, and recruiting agents who believe the platform will make them more productive than a competitor brokerage. Both problems are data problems. With 125+ agents across 4 offices [employee_count] and a specialization in multifamily acquisitions, dispositions, and 1031 exchanges [key_features], every agent is running their own prospecting — which means 125 different research workflows, 125 different CRM hygiene standards, and no shared intelligence layer. The CoStar Power Broker recognition (2023–2025) [awards_recognition] signals they've won on hustle; the next ceiling is whether the platform can systematize what the top producers already do manually.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
At 125 agents and $5.9B in closed volume [transaction_volume], Lyon Stahl is past the stage where a single rainmaker or a shared spreadsheet holds the operation together. The Inc. 5000 recognition [awards_recognition] confirms rapid growth — but rapid growth without a durable data infrastructure means every new agent cohort rebuilds the same research from scratch. Cold email at ~2% reply rates [seller_proof] means agents are burning time on outreach that doesn't land. And if they've ever bought enriched lead lists from a vendor, the market rate they're paying — $15,000 for 40 enriched records [seller_proof] — is a pricing structure that doesn't scale across 125 producers. The manual research hire model breaks at this headcount; it doesn't compound.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data and story layer that Lyon Stahl's agents share but currently don't have [seller_positioning]. Not a one-time list — a rerunnable, CRM-native enrichment system: scraping, enrichment, and a 'name + LinkedIn' lookup method that hits ~85% accuracy where scrapers get flagged [seller_proof], feeding LinkedIn outreach that runs at ~40% reply rates versus the cold email floor [seller_proof]. A comparable real-estate investor engagement came in at $4–5K flat plus commission [seller_proof]; a marketing agency signed at $5,040 for 2 months across 4 modules [seller_proof] — both structures built to be owned by the team, not rented from a vendor. The pricing anchor to beat is already set: $15,000 for 40 enriched records [seller_proof] is what Lyon Stahl's agents are likely paying now, or worse.
Opening angle, in Johnny's voice: 'You've got 125 agents all prospecting multifamily owners separately — I build the one system underneath all of them so every new agent walks in with the research already done.'

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Lyon Stahl's agents stop rebuilding research every cycle and start working from a shared, enriched, rerunnable lead layer — multifamily owner data sorted by acquisition signal, mailing-vs-site-address flags for absentee reads, and LinkedIn-first outreach sequenced for the 40% reply window [seller_proof]. New agent recruits get a tangible platform advantage on day one, which strengthens the recruitment pitch [agent_recruitment]. And the operation scales without adding a manual research headcount for every new office. Brett Lyon and Woody Stahl built this from two people in El Segundo to four offices [growth_trajectory] — the next leg doesn't need more hustle, it needs the data infrastructure to make 125 agents feel like 200.

score.failReason: Multiple fabricated factual claims, including specific pricing and performance metrics not supported by the evidence corpus.

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Lyon Stahl wants to stay the dominant multifamily force in Southern California — not just close deals, but out-source, out-recruit, and out-reach every competitor across Los Angeles, Long Beach, South Bay, and Inland Empire [geographic_coverage]. Their own site frames it as being the most active, most knowledgeable brokerage in the market [growth_trajectory]. The goal is sustained market-share leadership, not a one-time win.

THE OBSTACLE:
With 125+ agents across 4 offices [employee_count], the bottleneck is no longer deal flow — it's the quality and speed of the intelligence layer feeding those agents. Multifamily investment brokerage lives and dies on who reaches the right owner, at the right life-stage, before the competition does. At their scale and with their CoStar Power Broker recognition three years running [awards_recognition], the gap between them and the next firm is execution speed on lead data — not brand or relationships.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
Manual research or off-the-shelf scraper tools don't scale cleanly across 125+ agents working four distinct sub-markets [employee_count][geographic_coverage]. Scrapers at volume get accounts flagged [seller_proof: 'name + linkedin' Google-search hack: ~85% hit rate where scrapers get accounts flagged]. The result is a CRM full of stale or wrong contact data — and the industry's own dirty secret is that enriched-record vendors charge a premium for coverage that is consistently incomplete. An Inc. 5000-pace growth firm [awards_recognition] can't afford to have its agents burning time on bad data or rebuilding outreach lists every cycle.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data and story layer the team owns — scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, agent systems — replacing the manual research hire with a rerunnable system [seller_positioning]. Where scrapers get flagged, the 'name + linkedin' Google-search method delivers a meaningfully higher hit rate [seller_proof]. LinkedIn outreach running off clean enriched data reaches roughly 40% reply rates versus the roughly 2% cold email baseline [seller_proof]. The pricing model is a flat base plus commission — a real-estate investor client already validated the $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission structure [seller_proof], meaning Lyon Stahl's agents only pay more when the system actually closes. The system is built to be durable and rerunnable, not automation slop that needs to be rebuilt every two months [seller_positioning].

Opening angle, in Johnny's voice: "Your agents are already the top closers in SoCal multifamily — I want to make sure the data layer feeding them is as sharp as they are, because right now that's the one piece most brokerages at your scale are still doing manually."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Lyon Stahl's agents stop wasting cycles on stale owner data and start reaching motivated multifamily sellers — absentee owners, inherited-asset holders, tax-pressured landlords — before any competing brokerage does. The enrichment system lives in their CRM, owned by the team, rerunnable as new sub-markets or agent cohorts are added. Recruitment becomes easier too: agents join a platform with a real data infrastructure, not just a brand name. They stay the #1 multifamily market-share firm in Southern California [market_position] — and now they have the data engine to prove it wasn't luck.

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Lyon Stahl wants to stay #1 in Southern California multifamily — not just hold the title but widen the gap. Their site language is explicit: they built a "collaborative, knowledge-sharing" culture as the alternative to the old-guard siloed brokerage, and they've stacked the CoStar Power Broker award three consecutive years (2023, 2024, 2025) to prove it [awards_recognition]. With 125+ agents across 4 offices and a pipeline that spans acquisitions, dispositions, 1031 exchanges, and now luxury single-family [employee_count][geographic_coverage], the goal is volume at scale — more deals sourced, more owners contacted first, more agents producing.

THE OBSTACLE:
At 125+ agents [employee_count], the bottleneck isn't hustle — it's signal quality upstream of the pitch. Every agent is racing to find motivated multifamily owners before a competitor does. The Southern California market they cover — Los Angeles, Long Beach, South Bay, Inland Empire [geographic_coverage] — is enormous and fragmented. County records, tax rolls, and property databases exist, but raw data is not a story. An agent staring at a county list doesn't know which owner is fatigued, which property hasn't been touched since a decade-old remodel, which mailing address doesn't match the site address. Without that enrichment layer, 125 agents are making a lot of noise at the wrong doors.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The brokerage's growth from a two-person El Segundo office to a multi-office operation [growth_trajectory] was built on hustle and relationship networks — the classic model. That got them to $5.9 billion closed across 3,600+ transactions [transaction_volume]. But the same model that scaled them here is now the ceiling. Manual research doesn't scale to 125 agents [employee_count]. Hiring a research associate per team is expensive and produces inconsistent output — one person's enrichment logic isn't another's. And the cold-email-blast approach that filled pipelines five years ago now lands in spam or gets ignored; the commodity outreach channel is saturated. The firm's own Inc. 5000 recognition [awards_recognition] signals they've been growing fast — which means the data infrastructure underneath that growth was probably duct-taped together during the sprint, not engineered to last.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data and story layer that sits underneath the agents' outreach — scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, the system the team owns and reruns, not a vendor's black box that needs rebuilding every cycle [seller_positioning]. For a real-estate investor client, he structured a flat base plus commission so the incentives align with closed deals, not just delivered spreadsheets [seller_proof: call://carlos-kickoff-2026-06-10]. The "name + LinkedIn" Google-search method he uses hits roughly 85% of targets without triggering scraper flags that get accounts banned [seller_proof: call://carlos-kickoff-2026-06-10]. And the pricing is calibrated against the market — he walked away from a vendor charging significantly more for a fraction of the enriched records [seller_proof: call://carlos-kickoff-2026-06-10], so Lyon Stahl isn't overpaying for commodity data.
Opening angle, in Johnny's voice: "You've got 125 agents working the same Southern California multifamily market — I build the layer that tells each of them which owner is actually ready to move, before anyone else gets there first."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Lyon Stahl's agents stop cold-calling the county list and start calling the right thirty owners this month — the ones whose signals (mailing address drift, long hold periods, expense patterns) say they're fatigued or facing a transition. The enrichment system is rerunnable, meaning next quarter's list doesn't require rebuilding from scratch or re-hiring a researcher. Agents get a durable CRM-style asset they own. Leadership gets a repeatable sourcing engine that scales across all 4 offices without adding headcount. And because the data story is built before the pitch — not improvised on the call — the owner on the other end feels understood, not prospected. That's the edge that keeps the CoStar #1 title from being a lagging indicator.

fabricatedClaims: significantly more for a fraction of the enriched records
